title: How Oscar Winner Tamara Deverell Built the Beautifully Brutal World of Frankenstein
text: Tamara Deverell won her first Academy Award for production design on Guillermo del Toro's Frankenstein, sharing the honor with set decorator Shane Vieau. This marked her second Oscar nomination, following her work on Nightmare Alley. Deverell describes the film as a fantastic operatic ship, emphasizing how theatrical and operatic sensibilities guided the production design. A key tension in the production involved balancing del Toro's fantastical vision with Deverell's historically-minded approach. She insisted the film was fundamentally a period piece, even as it incorporated imaginative elements. The steam engines and battery towers were exaggerated but plausible for the Victorian Industrial Revolution era. Deverell emphasized that research isn't just going on the internet. She advocated for visiting libraries, traveling, observing nature, and studying historical catalogs. The Hunterian Museum in London proved crucial, providing authentic medical instruments and anatomical tables they recreated. The University of Glasgow's vaulted ceiling architecture influenced the creature's cell design. They filmed at Wilton House, the same location used for Kubrick's Barry Lyndon.
